contentBody: What does CSV stand for, and how are columns usually separated in a typical CSV file?
option.1: Comma-Separated Values; columns are separated by commas
option.2: Compressed Spreadsheet Values; columns are separated by tabs
option.3: Character-Separated Variables; columns are separated by semicolons only
option.4: Column-Sorted Values; columns are separated by pipes
answerExplanation: CSV is plain text: one row per line, fields often separated by commas; a header row with column names is common.

contentBody: After import pandas as pd, which call loads a local file data.csv into a DataFrame using the default comma separator and first row as column names?
answerExplanation: pd.read_csv() is the standard way to parse CSV text into a DataFrame.

contentBody: Which attributes or properties of a DataFrame df correctly describe its structure or contents?
option.1: df.shape returns the number of rows and columns as a tuple
option.3: df.values is a NumPy array of the data without column names in the array itself
option.4: df.dtypes returns a single scalar describing the dtype of the entire DataFrame
answerExplanation: df.dtypes is a Series (one dtype per column), not one scalar for the whole frame.

contentBody: What does df.info() primarily show you?
option.1: Only the first five rows of the DataFrame
option.2: A concise summary: index range, column names, non-null counts, dtypes, and approximate memory use
option.3: Only the list of column names, with no dtype or missing-value information
option.4: A correlation matrix for all numeric columns
answerExplanation: info() is a standard way to see structure and where values are missing (via non-null counts), along with dtypes.

contentBody: For row selection, which statements about .loc and .iloc are true?
option.1: .loc selects by label; .iloc selects by integer position
option.2: Label-based slicing with .loc is inclusive of both endpoints
option.3: Integer slicing with .iloc follows Python convention: the stop index is exclusive
option.4: .iloc[0] and .loc[0] always refer to the same row in every DataFrame
answerExplanation: If the index is not a default 0,1,2,... integer index or labels differ from positions, .loc[0] and .iloc[0] need not match.

contentBody: You want rows where Department is 'Engineering' and Salary is greater than 70000. Which expression is valid in Pandas?
answerExplanation: Use bitwise & and | with parentheses around each condition; Python and/or do not work element-wise on Series.

contentBody: A DataFrame has a column State with values like 'NY', 'NJ', 'CA'. You want all rows where State is one of 'NY', 'NJ', or 'CT'. Which approaches are appropriate?
answerExplanation: .isin() is idiomatic for many OR conditions; chained | with parentheses also works. Options C and D are invalid or wrong for Series element-wise checks.

contentBody: You call df.sort_values(by=['Department', 'Salary'], ascending=[True, False]). What does this do?
option.1: Sorts first by Department ascending, then by Salary descending within each department
option.2: Sorts only by Department and ignores Salary
option.3: Sorts Department descending and Salary ascending
option.4: Raises an error because two columns cannot be sorted together
answerExplanation: by lists columns in sort priority; ascending is parallel: first key A to Z, second key high to low per group.
